speaker: Guest
text: Basically Gmail, and then all the rest is in this tiny little part of the graph.

speaker: Guest
text: So we thought that after using Gmail, I used it since I don't know when I signed up, a few weeks into it, I got one of those invite codes.

speaker: Host
text: That was a really clever launch.

speaker: Guest
text: And I used it ever since.

speaker: Guest
text: So that's literally 17 years or something of that, of Gmail usage.

speaker: Guest
text: And over that time I built up a lot of opinions about things that didn't work quite like I would prefer it to work.

speaker: Guest
text: And we put all those opinions into a new software product.

speaker: Guest
text: Spent about almost two years developing it, millions of dollars in accumulative R&D funds, and launched it in the summer of 2020, which, by the way, time to launch a product, well, 2020 wasn't great for a whole host of different reasons.

speaker: Guest
text: We were kind of trying to slot in a...

speaker: Host
text: Can there just be a week where the whole world is not just insane?

speaker: Guest
text: We finally picked a week.

speaker: Guest
text: We went live, and then we had the battle of our lives with Apple.

speaker: Host
text: With Apple.

speaker: Host
text: I remember that.

speaker: Guest
text: And ultimately ended up...

speaker: Host
text: They didn't want to approve your app.

speaker: Guest
text: They didn't want to approve our app unless we paid the toll fee, the 30%.

speaker: Guest
text: And they were basically willing to say, you can't be in the App Store, which for an email product like that is a death sentence.

speaker: Host
text: Yes.

speaker: Guest
text: You have to be on not just mobile phones, but specifically the iPhone.

speaker: Host
text: This is true today.

speaker: Guest
text: S.

speaker: Guest
text: is the most affluent software market in the world.

speaker: Guest
text: So for that business to work, we needed to be on the iPhone.

speaker: Guest
text: After a two-week epic struggle back and forth, thankfully time to perfection with WWDC, where Apple preferably didn't want to look like the Goliath squashing a...

speaker: Host
text: Tiny David.

speaker: Guest
text: developer, a tiny developer.

speaker: Guest
text: We ended up being allowed in, and Apple sort of rewrote the rules after the fact to make it fit.

speaker: Guest
text: It was a small victory, not the ultimate victory, but at least it allowed us to be there.

speaker: Guest
text: And Hey ended up being an enormous success, in part, ironically, because Apple gave us wall-to-wall coverage for two weeks.

speaker: Guest
text: When I look back upon that, I think I wouldn't have gambled like that because the outcome would have been zero, right?

speaker: Guest
text: Like Apple refuses our app.

speaker: Guest
text: We sign up 200 people, and the app is dead.

speaker: Guest
text: What instead happened was they gave us a multi-million dollar launch campaign and coverage in all major media, and we signed up tens of thousands of people in those first weeks.

speaker: Guest
text: That was an insane event, but also very satisfying.

speaker: Guest
text: And the other satisfying thing was I just love Hey.

speaker: Guest
text: I use it every day.

speaker: Guest
text: I basically use Basecamp in terms of web applications.

speaker: Guest
text: That's where we do all our collaborative work.

speaker: Guest
text: And then my number two app—and many days, it's my number one app—is Hey because I just do all my stuff in email.

speaker: Guest
text: I am constantly communicating with people.

speaker: Guest
text: I'm writing.

speaker: Guest
text: I'm doing a lot of stuff in email, as many people do.

speaker: Guest
text: And having that be a pleasurable experience and a nice environment and my inbox being a little more sacred than what happens with Gmail where total strangers around the world can just make your pocket buzz if you have notifications turned on, which they are by default, just seems insane to me, right?

speaker: Guest
text: This idea that there's direct access to one of my most important daily priority lists.

speaker: Guest
text: Like anyone can put something on that?

speaker: Guest
text: Insane.

speaker: Guest
text: Anyway, Hey doesn't do that.

speaker: Guest
text: We have the screener and no one gets to reach your inbox before you've said, I want to hear from this person.

speaker: Guest
text: And most of the time I say no to most people, right?

speaker: Guest
text: Like things end up in the screener and we have thumbs up.

speaker: Guest
text: I will hear from this person.

speaker: Guest
text: Thumbs down.

speaker: Guest
text: I'll never hear from that person again.

speaker: Host
text: This is how I reached out.

speaker: Host
text: I mean, we were, I'm not sure we were connected on X, but I said email because your email is out there and your screener seems to have worked because it gave me the thumbs up.

speaker: Guest
text: It did because the screener is me.

speaker: Guest
text: So there's not even AI trying to suss out whether I want to hear from you or not.

speaker: Guest
text: Because what turns out to be true is it's actually not that onerous to once a day, go through your screener and say thumbs up or down because there aren't that many people in the world.

speaker: Guest
text: And if you say no to the annoying pestering salespeople who within Gmail managed to read your inbox seven times, then the workload is much less.

speaker: Guest
text: And it's very satisfying, I would say too, because when I was using Gmail, I would get roped into this sales tactic that they of course rely on, which is that like you write back and say like, no, thank you.

speaker: Guest
text: I'm not interested.

speaker: Guest
text: And then they would respond again.

speaker: Host
text: Yes.

speaker: Guest
text: And now you feel like, wait, am I now obligated to respond to this person?

speaker: Guest
text: I kind of feel like I am.

speaker: Guest
text: And occasionally I would end up writing.

speaker: Guest
text: And even if I wouldn't write, they still have access to my inbox.

speaker: Guest
text: So I would hear from them again.

speaker: Guest
text: Next week, they have a whole drip campaign.

speaker: Host
text: They all fucking do, right?

speaker: Guest
text: That any outreach is seven emails.

speaker: Guest
text: It's not one emails.

speaker: Guest
text: It's seven emails.

speaker: Host
text: And if you show any sign of life, it's probably 52.

speaker: Guest
text: That's just not how it works in Hey.

speaker: Guest
text: I say thumbs down one time, never hear from that person again.

speaker: Guest
text: It's actually amazing how quickly you can curate your garden from that.
